card.spark: Mathematics education research on dual-process cognition — the split between fast intuitive recognition and slow deliberate reasoning — maps onto the exact asymmetry your system is already exploiting: users can recognize a resonant connection before they can explain why it resonates.
card.bridge: Your system's scoring skeleton is designed to be read by System 1, not System 2 — a user glancing at a candidate resonance is not being asked to construct an argument, they are being asked whether something lights up. That asymmetry is load-bearing: if you forced users to articulate before they recognized, you would be demanding System 2 output from a System 1 signal, and most genuine connections would be discarded because they cannot yet be explained. The anchor's claim that 'recognition precedes articulation' is not a design preference — it is a description of how cognition actually sequences. This means the skeleton's job is not to be complete or precise; it only needs to be legible enough to trigger the recognition response, after which the user's own System 2 can do the articulation work downstream.
card.source_shape: Dual-process theory divides cognition into two modes: System 1, which is fast, automatic, and pattern-recognizing, and System 2, which is slow, deliberate, and articulable. The key structural fact is that these two systems are not just different speeds — they are different kinds of knowing. System 1 fires before System 2 has assembled the words, which means a person can reliably detect that something fits before they can tell you what it fits or why.
card.use: Consider auditing your card or surface design specifically against the System 1 legibility test: can a user form a gut read within two seconds of seeing the connection, before any deliberate analysis begins? If the presentation requires reading and reasoning before the resonance is felt, the design is asking System 2 to do System 1's job.
card.limit: The source paper is a deficit model — its entire purpose is to correct faulty intuitions in students by using structured intervention to move them from wrong toward right. That goal is structurally opposed to what your system is doing: you treat incompleteness as generative, not as an error to be fixed, and you want divergent surface area, not convergence on a correct answer. Any design patterns the paper offers for 'leveraging' dual-process theory are aimed at overriding unreliable System 1 responses, whereas your system is betting that System 1 recognition is exactly the faculty to trust. The bridge holds for the recognition-precedes-articulation mechanism, but breaks the moment you try to borrow the paper's instructional logic — that logic is built to suppress the very intuitions your system depends on.

unplaced.why_unplaced: evidence describes dual-process theory but does not document the claimed structural analogy between System 1/2 and the user's recognition-before-articulation mechanism

card.spark: A system that turns half-drawn physics diagrams into live simulations works because the skeleton is enough — the AI doesn't need the user to fully describe what they want, it generates a candidate and lets the user recognize value after the fact. Your system runs on the same asymmetry.
card.bridge: Your system's anchor works the same way: the user's concept is a skeleton, not a complete specification, and that incompleteness is the input rather than a problem to be solved before work begins. Just as Augmented Physics doesn't wait for the user to describe every dynamic property before generating — it acts on structural signals and surfaces a candidate — your wandering agents act on the anchor's partial shape to surface resonances the user couldn't have requested explicitly. The moment of value in both systems is recognition, not instruction: the user sees what the system produced and knows whether it lands. And in both cases, the skeleton being 'enough' is not a compromise — it is the mechanism that makes the whole thing work, because a fully specified input would eliminate the generative gap the AI is filling.
card.source_shape: Augmented Physics takes a static, incomplete diagram from a textbook — arrows, labels, rough shapes — and feeds it through AI models (Segment Anything for object detection, multimodal LLMs for interpretation) to produce a fully interactive simulation the user never explicitly specified. The user didn't describe the simulation; they drew a skeleton, and the system inferred the rest. Recognition happens at the output, not the input: the user sees the generated simulation and decides whether it captures what they meant.
card.use: Consider making the incompleteness of the user's anchor explicit in how you frame the system to users — not as a limitation they should resolve before starting, but as the signal the agents are designed to work from. A user who understands that their half-formed concept is already sufficient input may engage more honestly and get better results.
card.limit: Augmented Physics applies a tightly scoped, domain-specific pipeline to a single input: it knows it is working with physics, it knows what a diagram looks like, and its generative moves are bounded by that context. Your wandering agents operate with no such domain ceiling — they traverse all knowledge stochastically, looking for non-obvious resonances across wildly different fields. The serendipity and chaotic surface area that define your model have no analog in Augmented Physics, which never wanders and never surprises itself. The bridge holds for the skeleton-as-sufficient-input shape and the recognition-before-articulation asymmetry, but breaks entirely at the question of how unbounded, cross-domain search works.

unplaced.why_unplaced: evidence describes Augmented Physics but does not document the claimed structural analogy between the skeleton-to-simulation pipeline and the user's anchor

card.spark: A 1960s learning theory built to diagnose what a student doesn't yet know turns out to share a structural bone with your system: both treat an incomplete skeleton not as a failure state, but as the most useful signal available.
card.bridge: Your system's anchor plays the same structural role that Gagné's hierarchy plays for the assessment engine: it is a partial scaffold that tells the wandering agent what is already load-bearing, without dictating where to go next. When a user brings a half-formed concept, that incompleteness isn't a problem to be resolved before the search begins — it is the signal that directs the search, just as a student's gap pattern directs the Gagné-based diagnosis toward the missing rung. The correspondence holds at the level of mechanism: in both cases, a skeleton that is deliberately not a full map is precisely what makes productive next-step identification possible. Where Gagné asks 'which prerequisite is absent,' your system asks 'which resonance is absent' — and the half-knowledge is what makes the question answerable.
card.source_shape: Gagné's learning hierarchy arranges mathematical skills into a strict prerequisite chain — you cannot meaningfully attempt long division without first mastering subtraction, and subtraction without place value. An assessment system built on this hierarchy doesn't just score a student's answers; it reads the pattern of gaps to locate exactly which lower rung is missing. The skeleton of prerequisites is the diagnostic instrument — incompleteness is the data, not the noise.
card.use: Consider making the incompleteness of a user's anchor explicit and legible to the system — not something to be filled in before wandering starts, but a structured input that the agent reads as 'here is what is load-bearing, here is what is open' — treating the gap itself as the generative coordinate.
card.limit: Gagné's hierarchy is a fixed, linear chain inside a single domain: each rung is defined in advance, the order is non-negotiable, and the system never wanders outside mathematics. Your anchor is the opposite — deliberately incomplete, non-directive, and designed to enable chaotic traversal across all knowledge domains. The bridge holds for the idea that a skeleton is a sufficient and productive signal, but it breaks entirely at the question of how that skeleton is structured: Gagné's is deterministic and closed, yours is stochastic and open. Importing Gagné's logic of fixed prerequisites into your system would contradict the cross-domain, non-linear search that makes it work.

unplaced.why_unplaced: evidence describes Gagné's learning hierarchy but does not document the claimed structural analogy between the hierarchy skeleton and the user's anchor
